grammarLessons.name: Devoir & Il Faut — Must / Have To

cards.grammarNote:
To express pain: **avoir mal à** + body part.
j'ai mal à la tête · j'ai mal au dos · j'ai mal aux pieds
**à + le = au** · **à + les = aux**

**les cheveux** is always plural (the collection of hair); one strand = **un cheveu**.
With body parts, use the definite article, not a possessive: elle a les cheveux longs (not *ses cheveux longs*).

**le visage** is masculine: un beau visage, le visage humain.
**Lave-toi le visage** = wash your face — reflexive verb (**se laver**) + definite article for body part, not a possessive.
The reflexive pronoun (te) already tells us whose face it is, so French says **le visage** not *ton visage*.
Also common: **la figure** (informal for face) · **le faciès** (medical term).
**l'œil** (singular) → **les yeux** (plural) — completely different words, one of the most irregular plurals in French.

**l'oreille** is feminine: une oreille, les oreilles.
Elision: **l'oreille** (not *la oreille*).
**j'ai mal à l'oreille** = my ear hurts — **à + l'** (before vowel, no contraction needed).
**l'oreille interne/externe** = inner/outer ear · **avoir de l'oreille** = to have a good ear for music.

**le nez** is masculine: le nez froid, le nez rouge.
**j'ai le nez froid** = my nose is cold — note this structure: **avoir + article + body part + adjective** (literally 'I have the nose cold').
This construction is used for describing body states: j'ai les mains froides, j'ai le dos raide.
**le nez qui coule** = runny nose (literally 'the nose that runs').

**la bouche** is feminine: une bouche, la bouche ouverte.
**Ouvre la bouche** = open your mouth — imperative of **ouvrir** (irregular): j'ouvre, tu ouvres, il ouvre.
**garder la bouche fermée** = to keep one's mouth shut · **bouche à bouche** = mouth-to-mouth resuscitation.
Distinguish: **la bouche** (the mouth as opening) vs **la gueule** (informal/rude, animal mouth).

**la lèvre** (singular) → **les lèvres** (plural, usually used in pairs).
**elle a les lèvres rouges** = she has red lips — adjective **rouges** agrees with the feminine plural **les lèvres**.
With body parts + **avoir**, use the definite article: elle a **les** lèvres (not *ses lèvres*).
**lire sur les lèvres** = to lip-read · **le baume à lèvres** = lip balm.

**la dent** (singular) → **les dents** (plural).
**j'ai mal aux dents** = I have a toothache — **à + les = aux**.
Bodycare: **se brosser les dents** = to brush one's teeth (reflexive + definite article).
**une dent de sagesse** = a wisdom tooth · **avoir les dents longues** = to be ambitious (idiom).

**la langue** has two key meanings: the tongue (body part) and a language.
Context: **tirer la langue** (to stick out one's tongue) vs **parler une langue** (to speak a language).
**Tire la langue** = stick out your tongue — imperative of **tirer** (to pull/stick out), regular -er verb.
**la langue maternelle** = mother tongue · **une langue étrangère** = a foreign language.

**le cou** = the neck (the outer part, from head to shoulders).
Distinguish from **la gorge** (the throat, interior): j'ai mal à la gorge (sore throat) vs j'ai mal au cou (stiff neck).
**j'ai le cou raide** = my neck is stiff — **avoir + le + body part + adjective** construction.
**une écharpe** = a scarf (worn around the neck) · **la nuque** = the back of the neck.

**le bras** is masculine; the -s is silent in singular: le bras [brɑ].
**je me suis cassé le bras** = I broke my arm — reflexive passé composé (**se casser**) + definite article (not possessive) for the body part.
With reflexive verbs + body parts: use **le/la/les** not **mon/ma/mes**: je me suis lavé **les** mains, il s'est blessé **au** genou.
cards.phrase: Je me suis cassé le bras.

cards.grammarNote:
With reflexive verbs + body parts, use the definite article (not a possessive):
Lave-toi **les** mains · Je me suis cassé **le** bras
The reflexive pronoun (me/te/se) already makes clear whose body part it is.

**le doigt** = finger (also used for toe in informal speech, though **l'orteil** is precise).
**il s'est coupé le doigt** = he cut his finger — **se couper** (reflexive: to cut oneself) uses **être** in passé composé.
**le doigt** takes the definite article after the reflexive (not *son doigt*).
**montrer du doigt** = to point at · **un coup de doigt** = a touch · **les empreintes digitales** = fingerprints.

**la poitrine** = chest/breast area (general term for both men and women).
**une douleur à la poitrine** = chest pain — **à la** before feminine noun.
**avoir une douleur** = to have a pain — use **avoir** + indefinite article + douleur.
Alternative: **j'ai mal à la poitrine** (I have pain in my chest).
Related: **le thorax** (thorax, medical term) · **la cage thoracique** = rib cage.
cards.phrase: Il a une douleur à la poitrine.

cards.grammarNote:
**le dos** = the back (of the body): j'ai mal au dos · une douleur dans le dos.
**j'ai mal au dos** = my back hurts — **à + le = au** before masculine noun.
**le bas du dos** = the lower back · **le haut du dos** = the upper back.
**dans le dos** = in/on the back · **avoir quelqu'un dans le dos** = to have someone on your back (figurative).

**le cœur** = heart (the organ and the emotional/metaphorical heart).
Note the ligature **œ** (o + e combined) — this is a distinctive French character.
**mon cœur bat vite** = my heart is beating fast — possessive **mon** used here as a standalone noun (not with a reflexive verb), so **mon** is natural.
Idioms: **avoir le cœur gros** (to be sad), **de tout cœur** (wholeheartedly), **par cœur** (by heart/from memory).

**l'estomac** = the stomach (the organ); **le ventre** = the belly/abdomen (broader term).
Note: to say 'my stomach hurts', French uses **j'ai mal au ventre** (not *à l'estomac*) in everyday speech.
**le ventre** is more common: j'ai mal au ventre · **le mal de ventre** = stomachache.
Elision: **l'estomac** (not *le estomac*) — the -c is silent: [lɛs.tɔ.ma].

**le muscle** is masculine: un muscle, les muscles.
**je me suis froissé un muscle** = I pulled a muscle — reflexive passé composé using **être**.
**se froisser** = to pull/strain (a muscle) · **se déchirer** = to tear (more severe): je me suis déchiré un muscle.
Note **un** (indefinite article) here instead of **le** — because it's an indefinite injury, not a specific named muscle.
cards.phrase: Je me suis froissé un muscle.

cards.grammarNote:
**la taille** has two meanings: waist (body part) and size (clothing size).
Context: **avoir la taille fine** (to have a slim waist) vs **quelle est votre taille ?** (what is your size?).
**la taille fine** — adjective **fine** follows the noun (default position for descriptive adjectives).
**fine** (fem.) = slim/slender (masc.: **fin**).
**la taille** also means height for people: **quelle est ta taille ?** (how tall are you?).

**la peau** is feminine: une peau douce, la peau sèche.
Plural is irregular: **les peaux** (not *les paux*).
**elle a la peau douce** = her skin is soft — **avoir + la + body part + adjective** construction.
**doux/douce** = soft (masc./fem.) · **sec/sèche** = dry · **gras/grasse** = oily.
Idiom: **être dans sa peau** = to be comfortable in one's own skin.

**le sang** is masculine: le sang rouge, une prise de sang.
The -g is silent: [sɑ̃].
**donner son sang** = to donate blood (possessive **son** is natural here — a voluntary act, not reflexive).
**une prise de sang** = a blood test (blood draw) · **le groupe sanguin** = blood type.
**avoir du sang froid** = to keep one's cool (idiom: literally 'to have cold blood').

**le cerveau** is masculine: un cerveau, le cerveau humain.
Plural: **les cerveaux** (regular -eau → -eaux pattern: gâteau → gâteaux, bateau → bateaux).
**le cerveau est complexe** — **est** is the third-person singular of **être** (to be).
Related: **le cervelet** (cerebellum) · **le cerveau** (brain/mind figuratively: c'est le cerveau de l'opération = he's the brains of the operation).

**le crâne** is masculine: un crâne, le crâne humain.
Note the circumflex accent on the â — a common feature in French, often indicating a historical dropped 's' (Latin *cranium*).
**protéger** = to protect — **protège** (3rd person singular present): le crâne protège le cerveau.
**se cogner le crâne** = to bang one's head · **chauve** = bald (describing the skull being visible).

**le sein** is masculine: un sein, les seins.
**ressentir une douleur dans** + body location = to feel pain in.
**elle a ressenti** — passé composé of **ressentir** (to feel/experience), conjugated with **avoir**: j'ai ressenti, tu as ressenti.
**dans la poitrine** = in the chest — **dans** here indicates location inside the body.
Note: **la poitrine** is often used as a more general alternative to **le sein** in medical or polite contexts.
cards.englishPhrase: She felt pain in her chest.
cards.phrase: Elle a ressenti une douleur dans la poitrine.

cards.grammarNote:
**la jambe** is feminine: une jambe, les jambes.
**j'ai la jambe fatiguée** = my leg is tired — **avoir + la + body part + adjective** structure.
**fatigué(e)** agrees with **la jambe** (feminine) → **fatiguée**.
**se casser la jambe** = to break one's leg · **les jambes en coton** = weak/shaky legs (idiom: literally 'cotton legs').
**la jambe** = the whole leg; **la cuisse** = thigh · **le mollet** = calf · **le genou** = knee.

**le pied** (singular) → **les pieds** (plural, regular).
**j'ai mal aux pieds** = my feet hurt — **à + les = aux**.
Common idioms: **mettre les pieds quelque part** (to set foot somewhere) · **de la tête aux pieds** (from head to toe) · **avoir les pieds sur terre** (to be down-to-earth).
**le pied** = foot (body) and also used in measurement: un pied (a foot, ~30cm).

**l'orteil** is masculine: un orteil, les orteils.
Elision: **l'orteil** (not *le orteil*).
**il s'est cogné l'orteil** = he stubbed his toe — **se cogner** (reflexive: to bump/stub) uses **être** in passé composé.
**se cogner** + article + body part: the reflexive already signals whose toe, so use **l'orteil** not *son orteil*.
**le gros orteil** = the big toe · **le petit orteil** = the little toe.

**l'articulation** is feminine: une articulation, les articulations.
Elision: **l'articulation** (not *la articulation*).
**mes articulations sont raides** = my joints are stiff — possessive **mes** (my, pl.) + noun + verb **être** + adjective.
**raide** = stiff (invariable in gender): raide (m./f.) · raides (pl.).
Related: **articulaire** (adj.: joint-related) · **l'arthrite** (fem.) = arthritis.

**le souffle** = breath (as air expelled) or a breath of air.
Distinguish from **la respiration** (breathing as a process) and **un souffle** (a puff of air).
**retenir sa respiration** = to hold one's breath (literally 'to hold one's breathing').
**le souffle** is also used figuratively: le souffle de la vie (the breath of life), être à bout de souffle (to be out of breath).
**couper le souffle** = to take one's breath away.

**voir** = to see (irregular verb): je vois, tu vois, il voit, nous voyons, vous voyez, ils voient.
**je vois clairement** — adverb **clairement** (clearly) formed from adjective **clair** + **-ment**: clear → clearly.
Note: **voir** vs **regarder**: **voir** = to see (involuntary/natural) · **regarder** = to look at (deliberate): je vois la montagne · je regarde la télé.
**voire** ≠ **voir** — **voire** means 'or even' (written language).

**entendre** = to hear (regular -re verb): j'entends, tu entends, il entend, nous entendons.
**je ne t'entends pas** — negation: **ne … pas** wraps around the verb: je **ne** t'entends **pas**.
Note: **entendre** vs **écouter**: **entendre** = to hear (passive/natural) · **écouter** = to listen (active): j'entends la musique · j'écoute la musique.
**s'entendre** = to get along: ils s'entendent bien (they get along well).

**sentir** = to smell (both: to smell something and to emit a smell).
**je sens les fleurs** = I can smell the flowers (perceiving the smell).
**ça sent bon** = it smells good · **ça sent mauvais** = it smells bad.
Note: **sentir** also means to feel (physical sensations): je sens la chaleur (I feel the heat).
**se sentir** (reflexive) = to feel a state: je me sens bien.

**goûter** = to taste (to sample/try food): goûte cette soupe.
Imperative (tu form): **goûte** — drop the -r from the infinitive for -er verbs.
Distinguish: **goûter** (to taste/sample) vs **avoir le goût de** (to taste of/like something): ça a le goût de citron (it tastes of lemon).
**le goût** = taste (the sense) · **un goûter** = an afternoon snack (especially for children).

**toucher** = to touch (regular -er verb).
Negative imperative: **ne touche pas à ça !** — note **à ça** (to that): **toucher à** = to touch something (with the preposition à): ne touche pas aux livres.
Simpler: **ne touche pas ça** (without à) is also heard informally.
**toucher** can also mean 'to move emotionally': ce film m'a touché (this film moved me).

**respirer** = to breathe (regular -er verb): je respire, tu respires, il respire.
Imperative: **respire** (tu form) · **respirez** (vous form).
**respire lentement** — adverb **lentement** (slowly) from **lent** (slow) + **-ment**.
**une grande respiration** = a deep breath · **retenir sa respiration** = to hold one's breath.
**respirer** is also used figuratively: respirer la santé (to radiate health).

**plier** = to bend/fold (regular -er verb): je plie, tu plies, il plie.
Imperative: **plie** (tu form) — used here: plie les genoux.
**les genoux** = the knees (plural of **le genou**; note irregular plural: **genou** → **genoux**).
**plier les genoux** = to bend the knees · **se plier** = to bend oneself/comply: se plier aux règles (to comply with the rules).

**s'étirer** = to stretch (reflexive verb).
Imperative (positive): **étire** + reflexive pronoun → **étire-toi** (stretch yourself) or **étire les bras** (stretch your arms — without reflexive, as used here).
**étire les bras** uses the definite article: **les** bras (not *tes* bras), because the instruction is about 'the arms' generically.
**un étirement** = a stretch (noun) · **les étirements** = stretches (warm-up exercises).

**l'épaule** is feminine: une épaule, les épaules.
Elision: **l'épaule** (not *la épaule*).
**mon épaule me fait mal** = my shoulder hurts — literally 'my shoulder makes me hurt/pain'.
**faire mal à** = to hurt (someone): ça me fait mal (it hurts me), mon dos me fait mal.
This contrasts with **j'ai mal à l'épaule** (I have pain in my shoulder — another natural phrasing).

**le genou** → plural **les genoux** (irregular: -ou → -oux; same pattern: bijou → bijoux, caillou → cailloux).
**je me suis fait mal au genou** = I hurt my knee — reflexive passé composé of **se faire mal**.
**se faire mal** = to hurt oneself (general): je me suis fait mal · **à + le = au**.
Compare: **j'ai mal au genou** (my knee hurts — current state) vs **je me suis fait mal au genou** (I hurt my knee — event).
cards.phrase: Je me suis fait mal au genou.

cards.grammarNote:
**le coude** is masculine: un coude, les coudes.
**il s'est cogné le coude** = he bumped his elbow — **se cogner** (reflexive) uses **être** in passé composé.
Past participle **cogné** agrees with **il** (masculine singular) — no change needed here.
**le coude** in idioms: **jouer des coudes** (to elbow one's way through a crowd), **coup de coude** (a nudge with the elbow).

**contracter** = to flex/contract (a muscle).
**contracte tes muscles** — imperative (tu form): regular -er verb, drop -r.
Note: possessive **tes** is used here with an imperative directed at a named body action — this is natural when not using a reflexive verb.
**contracter** also means to contract (a disease or a deal): contracter une maladie, contracter un emprunt.

**cligner des yeux** = to blink (literally 'to wink the eyes').
**des yeux** = of-the eyes — **de + les = des** in this fixed expression.
**ne cligne pas des yeux** = don't blink — negative imperative: **ne** + verb + **pas**.
**cligner** is also used for: **cligner de l'œil** (to wink one eye) · **le clignotant** = a car indicator (turn signal).
Note: the plural **les yeux** (irregular: œil → yeux) appears in many fixed expressions.

**transpirer** = to sweat/perspire (regular -er verb): je transpire, tu transpires, il transpire.
**je transpire quand je cours** — **quand** (when) introduces a time clause in the present tense.
Note: **suer** is a more colloquial/informal synonym: je sue (I'm sweating).
**la transpiration** = perspiration/sweat (noun) · **en sueur** = sweaty (adjective phrase): être en sueur.

**saigner** = to bleed (regular -er verb): je saigne, tu saignes, il/elle saigne.
**la blessure saigne** = the wound is bleeding — in French, the present tense covers both 'bleeds' (simple) and 'is bleeding' (continuous).
**une blessure** = a wound/injury · **saigner du nez** = to have a nosebleed (literally 'to bleed from the nose').
**le sang** (blood) is related: saigner → sang.

**se sentir** = to feel (a state or condition) — always reflexive: je me sens, tu te sens, il se sent.
**comment tu te sens ?** = how do you feel? — informal **tu** form.
Formal: **comment vous sentez-vous ?** (inversion for formal question).
**se sentir** + adjective: je me sens bien/mal/fatigué(e).
Distinguish: **sentir** (non-reflexive) = to smell or feel a physical sensation: je sens le froid.

**penser** = to think (regular -er verb): je pense, tu penses, il pense.
**à quoi tu penses ?** = what are you thinking about? — **à quoi** (about what) + **penser à**.
Note: **penser à** (to think about/of): je pense à toi · **penser de** (to think of/have an opinion): qu'est-ce que tu penses de ce film ?
Also: **je pense que** + indicative = I think that: je pense que c'est vrai.

**bouger** = to move (regular -er verb with a spelling consideration: -ger verbs add e before -ons: nous bougeons).
**je ne peux pas bouger le bras** — **ne … pas** + **pouvoir** + infinitive = can't do something.
**pouvoir** is irregular: je peux, tu peux, il peut, nous pouvons, vous pouvez, ils peuvent.
**bouger** = to physically move a part of your body or yourself · **déménager** = to move house.
cards.phrase: Je ne peux pas bouger le bras.

cards.grammarNote:
**osseux** = bony (masculine); feminine: **osseuse** (-x → -se).
**il a les mains osseuses** — body description: **avoir + les + body part + adjective**.
**osseuse** agrees with **les mains** (feminine plural) → **osseuses**.
**les os** = bones (singular: **l'os** [lɔs], plural: **les os** [le zo] — liaison makes the s audible in plural).
**osseux** also appears in medical terms: le tissu osseux (bone tissue).

**poilu** = hairy (masculine); feminine: **poiluе** (regular -e added).
**il a les bras poilus** — body description with **avoir**: **les bras** (masc. pl.) + **poilus** (masc. pl.).
**le poil** = body hair (one strand); **les poils** = body hair (collective/plural) — different from **les cheveux** (head hair).
Historical note: **un Poilu** (capital P) = a French WWI soldier (nicknamed for their unshaven appearance).

**doux** = soft/gentle (masculine); feminine: **douce** (-x → -ce, irregular).
**le tissu est doux** = the tissue/fabric is soft — adjective follows **être**.
Note: **doux/douce** also means gentle, mild, or sweet: une voix douce (a soft voice), un temps doux (mild weather), un caractère doux (a gentle nature).
**doucement** = gently/softly (adverb) · **la douceur** = softness/gentleness (noun).

**douloureux** = painful/sore (masculine); feminine: **douloureuse** (-x → -se).
**endolori(e)** = achy/sore (past participle used as adjective): mes muscles sont endoloris.
**endolori** is often preferred for muscles after exercise; **douloureux** for more intense pain.
**la douleur** = pain (noun); **douloureusement** = painfully (adverb).
Note: **endolori** agrees: endolori (m.) · endolorie (f.) · endoloris/endolories (pl.).

**gauche** = left (invariable adjective: same for masc. and fem.).
**le genou gauche** = the left knee — adjective follows the noun (most adjectives in French come after).
**je me suis blessé(e) au genou gauche** — reflexive passé composé of **se blesser** (to injure oneself): **être** auxiliary.
Past participle **blessé(e)** agrees with the subject.
Opposite: **droit(e)** = right · **à gauche** = to the left (direction) · **à droite** = to the right.
cards.phrase: Je me suis blessé(e) au genou gauche.

cards.grammarNote:
**droit** = right (direction/side) — adjective: masculine **droit**, feminine **droite**.
**le bras droit** = the right arm — adjective follows the noun.
**je me suis cassé le bras droit** — reflexive passé composé of **se casser** using **être**.
Note: **droit** also means 'straight' (tout droit = straight ahead) and is a homophone of **un droit** (a right/entitlement).
**à droite** = to the right (directional adverb).
cards.phrase: Je me suis cassé le bras droit.

cards.grammarNote:
**supérieur** = upper/superior — masculine; feminine: **supérieure**.
**la partie supérieure du corps** = the upper body — **du** = **de + le** (of the).
Adjective position: **supérieur** follows the noun here: la partie **supérieure**.
**du corps** = of the body — **de + le corps**.
Opposite: **inférieur(e)** = lower/inferior.
Also used figuratively: un grade supérieur (a higher rank), un air supérieur (a condescending manner).
cards.phrase: La partie supérieure du corps est forte.

cards.grammarNote:
**inférieur** = lower/inferior — masculine; feminine: **inférieure**.
**le bas du dos** = the lower back — **le bas** (the bottom/lower part) + **du dos** (of the back).
Note: French uses **le bas du dos** rather than *le dos inférieur* for 'lower back' in everyday speech.
**avoir mal dans le bas du dos** = to have lower back pain.
**dans** here = in (location), rather than the usual **à** for body pain.
cards.phrase: Il a mal dans le bas du dos.

cards.grammarNote:
**le corps** is masculine: un corps, le corps humain.
The final -s is silent: [kɔʁ].
**le corps humain** = the human body — adjective **humain** follows the noun.
**humain(e)** = human (adjective) · **un être humain** = a human being.
**le corps** in expressions: **garder le corps** (body and soul), **le corps enseignant** (the teaching profession), **le corps d'armée** (army corps).

**humain** = human (adjective) — masculine; feminine: **humaine**.
**la biologie humaine** = human biology — adjective follows the noun, agreeing with **la biologie** (feminine) → **humaine**.
**Ça fait partie de** = that's part of / it's part of — common phrase: ça fait partie de la vie (it's part of life).
Note: nationality/descriptive adjectives like **humain** come after the noun; a few common adjectives (beau, grand, etc.) come before.
cards.phrase: Ça fait partie de la biologie humaine.
